metadata.summary: A decision graph for architectural documentation.

nodes.title: Build a compile-time framework
nodes.summary: Generate framework wiring before runtime so failures surface earlier and startup stays predictable.

nodes.title: Use a framework boundary
nodes.summary: Keep application code scalable and testable by centralizing conventions in a framework layer.

nodes.title: Kotlin is the implementation language
nodes.summary: The project is written in Kotlin, so integration points should fit Kotlin tooling and idioms.

nodes.title: Use KSP for compile-time integration
nodes.summary: KSP provides a Kotlin-native symbol processing API for code generation.

nodes.title: Runtime reflection
nodes.summary: A rejected implementation option that discovers wiring while the application starts.

edges.rationale: Kotlin gives enough type-system support and compiler tooling to express framework contracts without hiding application behavior.
A framework boundary creates a small set of conventions that can be checked and generated ahead of runtime.
Because Kotlin is fixed externally, the generation mechanism should understand Kotlin symbols without falling back to Java-only annotation processing.
Once framework wiring is moved to compile time, a symbol processor is needed to inspect Kotlin declarations and generate code.
Runtime discovery was considered, but it moves wiring mistakes to execution time and can slow cold start, so it conflicts with the chosen compile-time direction.
